title: Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
chief_complaint: Severe tearing pain in my lower back and abdomen. Came on suddenly while I was gardening. I feel like I'm going to pass out. I have high blood pressure and I used to smoke.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Distended, diffusely tender. Pulsatile mass in periumbilical region (expansile, laterally). Bruit over mass.
physical_exam.Back: Severe flank and back pain. Ecchymosis in left flank (Grey Turner sign - retroperitoneal hemorrhage).
physical_exam.Extremities: Cold, mottled. Femoral pulses weakly palpable bilaterally.
physical_exam.ATLS: A: patent. B: clear. C: Class III hemorrhagic shock (HR 130, BP 70, narrow pulse pressure). D: confused (GCS 13 - cerebral hypoperfusion).

hidden_data.ct_abdomen: Infrarenal AAA 10cm with retroperitoneal rupture. Large retroperitoneal hematoma displacing left kidney. Active contrast extravasation. No intraperitoneal rupture.
hidden_data.bedside_us: AAA 10cm with intraluminal thrombus. Retroperitoneal fluid.
hidden_data.labs: Hb 6.5 (acute blood loss), Lactate 8.5, INR 1.8 (coagulopathy of trauma), Platelets 80,000, Fibrinogen 120 (massive transfusion protocol indicated)

correct_diagnosis: Ruptured Infrarenal Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm with Retroperitoneal Hemorrhage and Hemorrhagic Shock
key_learning_points: AAA = abdominal aortic diameter >3cm. Rupture risk: 5-5.5cm (5-10% per year), >6cm (25% per year). Most common location: Infrarenal (95%)

Clinical triad (present in only 50%): Abdominal/back pain + Hypotension + Pulsatile abdominal mass. Rupture is often misdiagnosed as renal colic, diverticulitis, or musculoskeletal back pain. HIGH index of suspicion in elderly male with sudden severe back pain and risk factors
Diagnosis: Bedside ultrasound if unstable (99% sensitive for AAA). CT if stable. Do NOT delay OR for imaging in unstable patient with known AAA and suggestive presentation

Immediate management: 2 large-bore IVs, Type O negative blood, MTP (1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:Platelets). Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) until proximal control achieved - aggressive fluid resuscitation increases bleeding and mortality. EMERGENCY open repair (laparotomy + aortic cross-clamp) or EVAR if available and anatomically suitable

EVAR vs Open: EVAR has lower 30-day mortality and shorter ICU/hospital stay. Requires suitable anatomy (infrarenal neck >1.5cm, no severe angulation, patent iliac arteries). Convert to open if EVAR fails or anatomy unsuitable. Post-op: ICU, monitor for abdominal compartment syndrome (tense abdomen + elevated peak pressures + oliguria → decompressive laparotomy)

Complications: Death (50-80% overall, 50% die before reaching hospital), Renal failure, Mesenteric ischemia, Spinal cord ischemia (paraplegia), Abdominal compartment syndrome, Graft infection. Screening: One-time US for men age 65-75 with smoking history (USPSTF Grade B)
